# TaggingDocs

> The GTM & GA4 reference that should have existed from the start.

TaggingDocs is a comprehensive technical reference for Google Tag Manager (GTM) and Google Analytics 4 (GA4). It covers client-side and server-side GTM implementation, dataLayer specification, GA4 configuration and reporting, consent management, and ad platform integrations. The site also provides free interactive tools for building dataLayer code, generating BigQuery SQL queries, and testing regex patterns.

## Sections

- [Foundations](https://taggingdocs.com/foundations/): Core concepts of web analytics tagging — what GTM is, how tags work, data flow architecture.
- [Client-Side GTM](https://taggingdocs.com/client-side/): Complete guide to client-side Google Tag Manager — setup, triggers, variables, tags, tracking implementation, debugging, and container management.
- [Server-Side GTM](https://taggingdocs.com/server-side/): Server-side Google Tag Manager fundamentals, setup, client configuration, tag setup, advanced patterns, and operations.
- [DataLayer](https://taggingdocs.com/datalayer/): The dataLayer specification — structure, ecommerce events (GA4 recommended events), custom events, platform-specific implementations, and validation.
- [GA4](https://taggingdocs.com/ga4/): Google Analytics 4 — fundamentals, configuration, reporting, BigQuery export and querying, troubleshooting, and APIs.
- [Consent & Privacy](https://taggingdocs.com/consent/): Consent mode, cookie banners, GDPR/CCPA compliance, and privacy-respecting analytics implementation.
- [GTM Internals](https://taggingdocs.com/internals/): How GTM works under the hood — container loading, tag sequencing, runtime behavior.
- [Custom Templates](https://taggingdocs.com/templates/): Building custom tag and variable templates for GTM.
- [Security & Governance](https://taggingdocs.com/security/): Tag governance, CSP policies, container security, and access control.
- [Browser & Privacy](https://taggingdocs.com/privacy/): Browser tracking prevention (ITP, ETP), cookie restrictions, and their impact on analytics.
- [Ad Platforms](https://taggingdocs.com/integrations/): Integrating GTM with advertising platforms — Google Ads, Meta, TikTok, LinkedIn, and more.
- [Recipes](https://taggingdocs.com/recipes/): Ready-to-use implementation guides — ecommerce tracking, form tracking, scroll tracking, regex patterns, and more.
- [Resources](https://taggingdocs.com/resources/): Templates, checklists, guides, and reference materials.

## Interactive Tools

- [DataLayer Builder + Validator](https://taggingdocs.com/tools/datalayer-builder/): Build correct dataLayer.push() code for any GA4 event, or validate existing code against the GA4 specification.
- [BigQuery Query Generator](https://taggingdocs.com/tools/bigquery-generator/): Generate ready-to-use SQL queries for GA4 BigQuery export data with 15+ templates.
- [GA4 & GTM Regex Tester](https://taggingdocs.com/tools/regex-tester/): Test regex patterns against RE2 (GA4's engine) and GTM matching rules with common pattern templates.

## Key Topics

### GA4 Ecommerce Events
The dataLayer ecommerce section covers all GA4 recommended ecommerce events: view_item_list, select_item, view_item, add_to_cart, remove_from_cart, view_cart, begin_checkout, add_shipping_info, add_payment_info, purchase, refund, view_promotion, select_promotion. Each event page includes the complete dataLayer.push() code, required and optional parameters, and common implementation mistakes.

### GA4 BigQuery
The BigQuery section covers exporting GA4 data to BigQuery, schema structure (the nested event_params RECORD field), writing efficient queries with _TABLE_SUFFIX date partitioning, UNNEST patterns for event parameters and items, and session reconstruction from event-level data.

### Server-Side GTM
Server-side GTM documentation covers the architecture (client → server container → vendor endpoints), Cloud Run and App Engine deployment, custom client development, the Measurement Protocol, first-party data collection, and same-origin proxying for improved data quality.

### Consent Mode
Consent documentation covers Google Consent Mode v2 (required for EEA), the distinction between basic and advanced consent mode, integration with major CMPs (Cookiebot, OneTrust, Usercentrics), and server-side consent enforcement.

### Regex in GTM and GA4
GTM uses ECMAScript regex with full-match behavior in trigger conditions. GA4 uses the RE2 regex engine (no lookaheads, lookbehinds, or backreferences) with partial-match behavior in report filters. The regex tester tool validates patterns against the correct engine.
